sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 92, level 3.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at (25,25) - red block, approached from above (DOWN) - game NOT over! Red blocks are safe when approached vertically. Now need: DOWN to (31,25), LEFT to (31,19), DOWN to (37,19), DOWN to (43,19), RIGHT to (43,25) goal.
- Plan: Continue with DOWN, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT to reach goal.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 3: you have used 27 of about 34 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player is still at (25, 25) after trying DOWN, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN. The player only moved RIGHT to (25, 31). This means from (25, 25), the player cannot go DOWN or LEFT. The player can only go RIGHT.
So from (25, 25), the player is stuck. The only way is to go RIGHT to (25, 31), but (25, 31) is also a red hazard.
Wait, looking at the output:
- DOWN: player=(25, 25) - didn't move
- LEFT: player=(25, 25) - didn't move
- DOWN: player=(25, 25) - didn't move
- DOWN: player=(25, 25) - didn't move
- RIGHT: player=(25, 31) - moved!
So from (25, 25), the player can only go RIGHT. The player cannot go DOWN or LEFT.
This means (25, 25) is a dead end. The player went RIGHT to (25, 31), which is also a red hazard.
Now the player is at (25, 31). From here, the player can try to go DOWN, LEFT, or RIGHT.
But wait, (25, 31) is a red hazard. If the player goes LEFT from (25, 31), the player is approaching (25, 25) from the right. This might cause game over.
Let me think about this. The player is at (25, 31). From here, the player can go:
- DOWN to (31, 31)? But (31, 31) is B (black), so this might be blocked.
- LEFT to (25, 25)? This is approaching a red block from the right, which might cause game over.
- RIGHT to (25, 37)? This is moving away from the goal.
Let me try DOWN from (25, 31) to see if it works.
Actually, wait. Looking at the block list, I see:
- (25, 25): R (red hazard)
- (25, 28): g
- (25, 31): R (red hazard)
- (25, 34): g
- (25, 37): W
So (25, 25) and (25, 31) are red hazards, connected by (25, 28) g.
From (25, 31), the player can go RIGHT to (25, 37). Then from (25, 37), the player can go UP to (19, 37). But the player already tried this path and it led to a dead end at (25, 25).
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the player needs to go DOWN from (25, 31) to (31, 31). But (31, 31) is B (black), so this might be blocked.
Wait, looking at the block list, I see:
- (25, 31): R
- (28, 31): g
- (31, 31): B/W
So (25, 31) is connected to (31, 31) via (28, 31) g. And (31, 31) is B (black) or W (white).
From level 2, the player could move DOWN through gray blocks adjacent to black blocks. So the player should be able to move DOWN from (25, 31) to (31, 31) via (28, 31) g.
But wait, (31, 31) is B (black), which might block the player. Let me try DOWN from (25, 31).
Actually, let me re-examine the block list. I see:
- (31, 31): B id=0
- (31, 31): W id=31
This means there are two blocks at the same position (31, 31), one black and one white. The white block is likely on top of the black block, so the player can stand on (31, 31).
But from the previous attempt, the player tried to go UP from (37, 31) to (31, 31) and it caused game over. So (31, 31) is not a valid destination when approached from below.
But maybe the player can go DOWN from (25, 31) to (31, 31). Let me try this.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at (25,31) after moving RIGHT from (25,25). From (25,25), DOWN and LEFT were blocked. Need to try DOWN from (25,31) to (31,31), then LEFT to (31,19), DOWN to (37,19), DOWN to (43,19), RIGHT to (43,25).
Plan: Try DOWN from (25,31) to see if it works.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at (25, 31)
# Try DOWN to (31, 31)

action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
player_pos = None
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        cr = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        player_pos = (cr, cc)
        break
go = last_action_result.get('game_over', False)
done = last_action_result.get('done', False)
lc = last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)
print(f"DOWN: player={player_pos}, go={go}, done={done}, lc={lc}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
